key_insight: Older adults with mild TBI often recover well by 6 months, but outcomes are much worse in the oldest patients and those with pre-injury cognitive impairment.
clinical_relevance: GOSE may be a useful trial endpoint for geriatric mTBI because it captured more change over time than ADL or FAQ measures in this study.
method: Prospective cohort study at two U.S. level I trauma centers
Enrolled adults ≥65 years presenting within 72 hours of mild TBI with head CT
Assessed GOSE at 2 weeks and 6 months, with weighting to reduce attrition bias
evidence_signal: In 253 participants, 6-month outcomes showed 14% mortality, 73% home independence, and 17% complete recovery, with better results in younger-old and cognitively unimpaired groups.
watch_item: Single-center pilot plus later two-center enrollment, substantial unknown pre-injury cognitive status, and attrition-adjusted analyses mean results may not generalize to all older mTBI patients.
paper_title: 6-Month Recovery after Mild Traumatic Brain Injury in Older Adults: A TRACK-GERI Study
